column2: Are there any plans to add more optional gradient channels to PulSeq, for example to control auxiliary gradient coils, matrix shim coils, etc., especially when these coils are capable of dynamic field control?
column4: Control of additional gradients was the initial motivation for getting pulseq started. In our older internal versions this is supported. However, more recent gradient functions are not supported, yet. If there is a clear need from the community, we have to discuss including this feature in the official release.

column2: Are there any plans to add more optional gradient channels to PulSeq, for example to control auxiliary gradient coils, matrix shim coils, etc., especially when these coils are capable of dynamic field control?
column4: Thank you Sebastian.  Currently we use a trigger from PulSeq to synchronize our extra encoding channels, which works OK, so there is not an urgent need for this.  But it could be very convenient to have all the linear and nonlinear gradient waveforms set up in PulSeq for ease of plotting together and working out the exact timing, etc.   But I realize this might have a small user base (at least for now).

column2: Are there any plans to add more optional gradient channels to PulSeq, for example to control auxiliary gradient coils, matrix shim coils, etc., especially when these coils are capable of dynamic field control?
column4: Interestingly enough, Thomas Roos has implemented hist own way of controlling additional channels, so I'd say we should connect together to see  how we proceed there. I was never too hapy about the way our internal implementation works, so it could be a good opportunity to revise it.

column2: Are the FOV and rotation changes coupled to the graphical prescription on-scanner ?
column4: For the matlab fov positioning code , we can apply shift/rotation of the fov to the sequence and generate the modified sequence and with that we dont need to change the fov window in the UI. this function supports both cartesian and non-cartesian sequences. We have also implemented it into the siemens pulseq interpreter and with that you can shift/rotate the fov window in the UI and it modifies your sequence and then run it. although the implemented function in the interpreter is only for sequences with constant gradient during the RF and ADC and we will extend it to support sequences with time-variable gradients during RF and ADC.

Perhaps I did something wrong...

The first 40-50us of ADC often is very oscillating when I am trying to implement cone like trajectory.

column4: I think this is due to digital filtering of the receiver. We start the adc a little bit before the read gradient for all center out trajectories and apply adc padding prior to reconstruction. I would be intersted if there is a general rule of thumb of how many adc samples are "corrupted" ?

column2: Do you include hight and weight of the subject in the model of SAR calculation? Is it gender and age sensitive?
column4: on GE, the routine is proprietary and I think it takes coil model and weights from UI (but that is not part of the API, it is done under the hood)

column2: Are the PNS and SAR calculations implemented for X-nuclei? I guess it would be for PNS since it only requires the gradients you designed but for SAR?
column4: we don't have real SAR calculation, because it requires coil and object models,we can only calculate b1_rms and mean relative RF power. However, I have heard that PyPulseq integrates with some advanced SAR toolbox, which is even capable of parallel transmit SAR calculations. Maybe somebody from the PyPulseq team (or Sydney) can answer that better

column2: Are the PNS and SAR calculations implemented for X-nuclei? I guess it would be for PNS since it only requires the gradients you designed but for SAR?
column4: Yes, the RF shimming developed in Erlangen for PyPulseq can calculate pTx-based SAR with Q-matrices (or virtual observation points, VOPs)

column2: Is the Siemens scanner able to predict SAR and PNS for a Pulseq sequence prior to running it?
column4: We are able to predict PNS in Matlab before running the sequence. As for SAR - the Siemens built-in model is used, so there are no safety concerns.

column2:
As far as I see, there are tv6, pge+pulceq, and the pulseg we learnt this week. 

Is it possible that we find a standard/recommended example that depicts PGE workflow, which also updates upon changes?
column4: tv6 is legacy and unsupported, pge+pulseg are a decoupling of pulceq, but for now functionally equivalent (pulseg is the internal sequence representation of pulceq, while pge is the validation library and serialization/deserialization for pulseq / pulseg conversion)

column2: How is the dynamic FOV positioning achieved in Pulseq on United Imaging? I don’t think this feature is implemented in other Pulseq interpreters, so this is really cool
column4: The FOV shift information will be stored in the protocol, and our interpreter will read this information and apply on the NCO of ADC and RF pulse. I believe other vendors also have the ability to achieve this, only that if they would like to spend the efforts

column2: Is there an equivalent function to calculate the spectrum of spiral waveform on PGE?
column4: for the moment, you can use the spectrum calculation from pulseq toolbox - provided that you verified conversion is accurate using pge toolbox

column2: Is there an equivalent function to calculate the spectrum of spiral waveform on PGE?
column4: The same seq.gradSpectrum() function can be used,  you just have to visually inspect the peaks and compare with the forbidden bands. We have discussed updating gradSpetrum() to accept forbidden bands directly without having to provide a .asc file (which is only for Siemens). Let's make sure that happens.

column2: what actually happens when setting the ‘flip angle’ option to ‘strict’ if the pulse amplitude exceeds the sequence limit? does the pulse become longer?
column4: if the pulse amplitude exceeds the system limit, the ‘strict’ option will disppear. If the “flip angle” is set to be adaptive mode, the pulse duration will become longer.

column2: what actually happens when setting the ‘flip angle’ option to ‘strict’ if the pulse amplitude exceeds the sequence limit? does the pulse become longer?
column4: if you cannot see the option "strict" for your sequence it means that the flip angles are not realizable and are readily "trimmed".

column2: We have an general workflow issue on our Siemens FreeMax, Sola and Vida scanners (tested with v1.5.0 and v1.5.1). After running a pulseq sequence, we cannot copy the sequence in the MRI protocol and rerun it. Sometimes the GUI crashes (requiring a workplace restart) and even if it does not crash, all parameters (fov positioning, shim, selected seq file) are lost and we need to set everything again. We have not yet identified a clear pattern of what exactly causes the crashes. The results from the executed scans are perfectly fine though.
column4: I typically use "copy-an-go" button, which I find more reliable and also more responsive because it avoids closing and reloading the sequence.

column4: Do you have any more specific insights in what causes workspace crashes generally/what to avoid? Is it sequence or setting specific?

column2: Could you explain the differences between implementing rf shiming and ptx?
column4: RF shimming is a pTx technique in which only the phase/amplitude differs for each channel, but the general pulse shape stays identical. With full pTx, you can also have independent waveforms on each channel

column2:
Sorry, I may have been a little late for this question. But I wanted to know about Siemens axis orientation vs pulseq axis orientation. https://github.com/pulseq/pulseq/discussions/162
I want to know if there is a way to match both the axis  system or do we still need manual rotation?  Does the new FOV positioning feature be used in this case?
column4: There is a way to match the axis, but ou have to be quite attentive to details.  The short versionof the answer is that the default conversion is calculated such that the Pulseq sequence logical xyz axes are mapped to the scanners physical xyz axes in the transversal orientation. This is quite different to the behaviour of the IDEA sequences though, which use a different mapping.
